html:
Hock Soon Capital (5346.KL): The Broken IPO Is Now a Cheap Capacity Option on Malaysia's Egg Market

The first correction is the most important one: Hock Soon is not a palm oil refiner. It is a Perak-based layer poultry operator producing ordinary and premium table eggs under QPlus and customer/private labels.

The stock is optically hated for good reasons. It listed on Bursa Malaysia's Main Market on Feb. 13, 2026 at RM0.60, opened at RM0.53, and the latest displayed quote is RM0.360, implying a market cap of about RM180 million on 500 million shares. The retail narrative is "failed IPO, weak subscription, subsidy profits gone." That is exactly why the setup is interesting.

Hock Soon's FY2025 reported profit was not clean. The Edge notes that egg subsidies were RM40.12 million in FY2025, equal to 95.5% of reported net profit, and adjusted net profit excluding subsidies fell to RM11.47 million. That makes the bearish reaction understandable. But the market has gone from correctly haircutting stale subsidy earnings to pricing the stock as if the post-subsidy business has no earnings power.

The latest operating evidence says otherwise. In Q1 FY2026, the company reported RM47.7 million revenue, RM16.2 million gross profit, RM8.4 million PAT, 34% gross margin, and RM8.7 million operating cash flow. The quarter still included RM3.65 million of final egg subsidy entitlement, so it is not perfectly clean. But even if that subsidy is stripped after tax, quarterly PAT is roughly RM5.6 million, or about RM22 million annualized on the enlarged share count. That is still only about 8x earnings at RM0.36, before giving Hock Soon any credit for capacity growth, Singapore exports, or balance sheet cash.

The stock is cheaper on enterprise value. At Dec. 31, 2025, before IPO completion, Hock Soon had RM56 million of cash and current investments against RM20.4 million of borrowings. The IPO then brought in RM60 million gross, mostly earmarked for Teluk Intan expansion. Pro forma, this is effectively a net cash poultry producer with a market cap near RM180 million and a forward earnings range that credible analysts put around RM31 million to RM38 million for FY2026-FY2027. That is not a stretched consumer staple multiple. It is a low-single-digit EV/earnings multiple for a profitable food producer funding a near-doubling of output.

The business is simple but not low quality. Hock Soon operates an integrated farm in Bidor, with over 30 closed-house coops, in-house feed milling, automated egg grading, and daily output capacity around 1.6 million eggs. TA Securities' IPO note highlights an 89.7% egg production rate in FY2025, mortality below 0.5% over FY2022-FY2025, and egg wastage below 1.5%. Table eggs represented roughly 97% of FY2025 revenue. This is not a promotional "brand story" with thin operations behind it. It is a scaled farm with cost control, automation, and distribution through wholesalers, retailers, and food manufacturers.

The transition is from subsidy distortion to market pricing. Malaysia fully ended egg subsidies on Aug. 1, 2025 after years of support, while price controls were lifted earlier. That creates uncertainty, but it also removes the ceiling on ASP recovery. Hock Soon's Q1 FY2026 average selling price was approximately RM0.36 per egg, versus FY2025 ASP of RM0.32 cited by TA. If current-cycle egg pricing holds, the subsidy removal is not just a profit hole. It is a price discovery event that lets efficient producers earn the spread between market egg prices and feed cost.

The main input risk is feed. Malaysia is heavily dependent on imported corn and soybean meal, and poultry feed prices reportedly rose in early 2026. That is the real bear case. But Hock Soon is not fully naked to spot feed shocks: it has in-house feed production, formulates multiple feed types internally, and said Q1 feed raw material costs were stable while a stronger ringgit helped procurement. The company's advantage is not immunity to corn and soy. It is better cost control than smaller, less integrated farms.

The upside torque is Teluk Intan. Hock Soon is using RM53.45 million of IPO proceeds toward a RM90 million new poultry farm on 21.3 hectares in Teluk Intan, with 25 closed-house coops, central grading and sorting, monitoring systems, and about 1.534 million eggs per day of incremental capacity over five years. Full completion would lift total daily capacity to roughly 3.16 million eggs, almost double the current base. A separate feed mill expansion is expected to lift feed capacity by 225 tonnes per day to 513 tonnes per day by late 2027. The current price is not valuing that expansion as a growth project. It is valuing it almost as a capital drain.

Singapore is the free option. Hock Soon has applied for Singapore Food Agency approval and underwent audit, with management previously targeting approval in 1Q2026. I have not seen a live filing confirming approval, so it should not be underwritten as a base-case fact. But if approval lands, it gives Hock Soon a premium export channel into a market that relies heavily on egg imports. Management has discussed Singapore as a potential 5% revenue contributor, and the signal value may matter more than the initial sales: SFA approval would validate product quality and help the company win new institutional customers.

What the market is missing is that there are three valuation layers. First, the existing Bidor farm appears capable of generating meaningful post-subsidy profit at current egg ASPs. Second, the balance sheet is overcapitalized post-IPO relative to the stock's market value. Third, Teluk Intan turns Hock Soon from a 1.6 million egg-per-day producer into a 3.1 million-plus egg-per-day platform, and investors are not paying for that option.

Analyst work is oddly supportive even where it is skeptical. TA Securities assigned only RM0.44 fair value at IPO, criticizing normalized historical earnings, but RM0.44 is still 22% above RM0.36. RHB's IPO note valued the stock at RM0.61 using 8x FY2027 EPS of 7.6 sen and projected FY2026-FY2028 net profit of RM35.7 million, RM38.1 million, and RM43.5 million. The current market price says both are too optimistic, while Q1 FY2026 already annualizes near the lower end of that range on reported earnings and remains profitable even after stripping the final subsidy.

Risks are real: feed inflation, ringgit weakness, disease outbreak, execution risk on RM90 million of capex, customer concentration through wholesalers, no formal dividend policy, 70% family control through Ong Legacy, related-party monitoring, and the possibility that regulators remain politically sensitive around egg prices. The bear case is not bankruptcy. It is that clean earnings settle closer to RM18 million to RM22 million, Teluk Intan consumes cash, and the market keeps the stock at a poultry discount.

That is not the base case at RM0.36. The base case is that Hock Soon earns through the post-subsidy reset, proves a 6.5-7.5 sen EPS run-rate by FY2027, and rerates back toward a normal small-cap food producer multiple as the market stops treating the IPO as broken paper. The bull case is that Teluk Intan and Singapore turn a cheap net-cash egg producer into a visible capacity compounder.

Targets from RM0.360 current price

Bear: RM0.28, down 22%. Clean EPS falls to 3.5-4.0 sen on feed pressure and weak ASP pass-through, and the market values the stock at 7x depressed earnings.

Base: RM0.66, up 83%. FY2027 EPS lands around 7.0-7.5 sen and the stock trades at 9x, still a modest multiple for a net-cash staple protein producer with funded capacity growth.

Bull: RM1.05, up 192%. Teluk Intan ramps without major cost overruns, Singapore approval opens a premium export channel, EPS moves toward 10-11 sen, and the market pays 9.5-10x for a larger, cleaner, post-subsidy earnings base.
